issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 29/06/26

✅ Implementation: Bruno API Documentation

This document provides a highly detailed, step-by-step implementation guide to execute the exhaustive tasks outlined in API_Documentation_Task_Plan.md. It covers environment configuration, scripting, testing, and CI/CD integration based on the api-collection/Panorra API Feeds directory.

1. Initial Configuration & Environments

Before documenting individual endpoints, we must accurately map out the environments in Bruno.

1.1 Environment Setup

  1. Open Bruno and navigate to the Environments tab for the Panorra API Feeds collection.
  2. Ensure the following environments are properly configured exactly as in the environments/ folder:
    • Local - Dev (Variables: base_url = http://127.0.0.1:9999)
    • Dev (Variables: base_url = https://dev-feeds.panorra.com)
    • Live (Variables: base_url = https://feeds.panorra.com)
  3. Add a global variable token (leave the value blank initially) to store the dynamically extracted JWT/Bearer token.

1.2 Global Headers

  1. Open the Collection Settings in Bruno.
  2. Under the Headers tab, set default headers for all requests (unless overridden by specific requests):
    • Content-Type: application/json
    • Accept: application/json

1.3 Authentication Handling (Pre-request Script)

To avoid manual token entry across numerous endpoints, use Bruno's pre-request script feature at the root collection level.

  1. Open Collection Settings > Script > Pre Request.
  2. Add the following snippet:
const token = bru.getEnvVar("token");
if (token) {
  // Assuming Bearer authentication. Adjust if the API uses a different auth scheme.
  req.setHeader("Authorization", `Bearer ${token}`);
}

2. Implementing the Initial Tokens (The Foundation)

Because this project (panorra-api-go) strictly implements GET methods, mutations like Login are handled externally. However, you will need a valid token to test the feeds API.

Example: Handling Authentication for Feeds Testing

You can manually inject the token in the environment variables, or use a helper endpoint from the mutation service if provided in the collection.

  1. Method: Obtain a token from the mutation API manually.
  2. Action: Copy the JWT and paste it into the Bruno environment variable token.

Alternatively, if there is a Generate Token.yml endpoint in the Centrifugo folder or similar:

  1. Method: GET
  2. URL: {{base_url}}/auth/token...
  3. Post-response Script (Token Extraction): 
    const resData = res.getBody();
if (resData && resData.data && resData.data.token) {
  bru.setEnvVar("token", resData.data.token);
  console.log("Token successfully extracted and saved to environment!");
}

3. Implementing Core Features (User, Posts, Notification, Search)

For modules containing dozens of files and subdirectories, standardization is key.

3.1 Standard Documentation Requirements

For every endpoint in these folders, the assigned developer must complete the following checklist:

  1. Path Variables: Use Bruno variables instead of hardcoded IDs.
    • Bad: GET {{base_url}}/users/detail?id=12345
    • Good: GET {{base_url}}/users/detail?id={{user_id}} (and define user_id in the environment or request vars).
  2. Query Parameters: Document all available filters natively in the Query tab so they are visible.
    • Example: ?limit=10&page=1&include=username,avatar
  3. Save Examples: This is crucial. Once an endpoint works against Local - Dev, click Save Example in Bruno. This caches the response payload, allowing front-end devs to mock the API without running the backend.

3.2 Testing GET Feeds and Search

Many endpoints in Posts, Elastic, and Search depend heavily on query string parameters for filtering and pagination.

  1. Map out all search parameters (e.g., search, sort, page).
  2. Include at least two saved examples: one for a successful search with results (200 OK) and one for an empty state (200 OK with data: []).

4. Background Tasks (QUEUE, Support, Centrifugo)

These endpoints often have different authentication requirements or bypass standard user auth.

  1. QUEUE Endpoints: If they require a specific server-to-server secret or IP whitelisting, document this in the endpoint's Notes tab in Bruno. If a specific header is required (e.g., X-Cron-Secret), add it to the request headers.
  2. Centrifugo: Document the WebSocket token generation payload precisely in the Centrifugo folder endpoints.

5. Export and CLI Integration (CI/CD)

Once the documentation is robust and tests are written, integrate it into your automated pipelines.

  1. Install Bruno CLI globally: 
    npm install -g @usebruno/cli
  2. Run the collection locally (for testing): 
    bru run --env "Local - Dev"
  3. Generate Static HTML Documentation: 
    bru export --format html --output api-docs.html
  4. CI/CD Pipeline (GitHub Actions Example):
    Create a workflow file .github/workflows/bruno.yml: 
    name: Run API Tests
on: [push]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
      - run: npm install -g @usebruno/cli
      - run: bru run --env Dev

Exhaustive checklist for building, organizing, and maintaining the API documentation using Bruno, based on the exact contents of the api-collection/Panorra API Feeds directory.

Phase 1: Setup and Environment Configuration

  • Initialize Bruno Collection: Verify/refine opencollection.yml.
  • Configure Environments: Local - Dev → http://127.0.0.1:9999, Dev → https://dev-feeds.panorra.com, Live → https://feeds.panorra.com.
  • Global Setup: Configure global headers (Content-Type, Accept).
  • Authentication Handling: Pre-request scripts / collection-level auth to inject Bearer tokens for protected routes.

Phase 2: Additional

  • Root: Additional Post, List Version.

Phase 3: Affiliate

  • Root: List Affiliate Categories, List Affiliate.

Phase 4: Broadcast

  • Root: Detail Broadcast Post, List Broadcast Bookmark, List Broadcast.

Phase 5: Category

  • Root: Categories.

Phase 6: Centrifugo

  • Root: Generate Token, Typing Comment.

Phase 7: Comparation

  • Root: Get All Post Timeline, Timeline, V1 Timeline.

Phase 8: Elastic

  • Root: Get Folder Bookmark, Get Posts, Get Reply Comment, Get Users.

Phase 9: Hashtag

  • Root: Search Hashtag Post, Search Hashtag.

Phase 10: Likes

  • Root: Check Like Post, List Like Comment, List Like Post, Total Block Like Post.

Phase 11: Mention

  • Root: Search Mention User.

Phase 12: Notification

  • Root: List Notification Month, List Notification, Total Unread Notification.

Phase 13: Posts

  • Sub-modules: Collections, Comments, Draft, Filter Custom, Folder Bookmark, Location, Multiple Post, Tagged Posts.
  • Collections: Collection Post Detail, List Collection Post.
  • Comments: List Comments, List Prev Replies Comment Copy, List Previous Comment, List Replies Comment, Total Block Comment, Total Block Like Comment.
  • Draft: Check Status Upload Post, Detail Draft Post, List Draft Posts, Validate Upload Panorama.
  • Filter Custom: Filter Custom.
  • Folder Bookmark: List Bookmarks, List Folder Bookmark.
  • Location: List Places, Places Detail Bookmarks, Places Detail Contributor, Places Detail Post, Places Detail, Search Suggest Places Detail Post, Search Suggest Places.
  • Multiple Post: Multiple Post.
  • Tagged Posts: List Tagged Posts.
  • Root: List Blocked Post, List Post Profile, Most Popular Post Feed, Most Popular Post, Nearest Post For Views, Nearest Post Using lat, lng, Post Aggregate, Post Detail, Post Timeline Just For You, Post Timeline Recent, Total Block Post.

Phase 14: QUEUE

  • Root: consume edit post, consume update score post.

Phase 15: Search

  • Root: Auto Complete Search Location, Search Folder Bookmark, Search Histories, Search Posts, Search Profile Follower, Search Profile Following, Search Users.

Phase 16: Support

  • Sub-modules: Comments, Posts, Users.
  • Comments: Detail Comment, List Comments, Report Comment Detail, Report Comment.
  • Posts: Detail Post, List Posts, Report Post Detail, Report Post.
  • Users: Detail User, List Users, Report User Detail, Report User.
  • Root: Report Bug.

Phase 17: User

  • Sub-modules: Follow, Streeet View.
  • Follow: Profile Follower, Profile Following.
  • Streeet View: List Account Street View.
  • Root: Base Image, Check Password, Check Support, Check Token, List Blocked Users, List User Account, Profile User by sid, Profile User, Suggestion User Half, Suggestion User More, Total Block Profile User, User Detail.

Phase 99: Enhancement and Automation

  • Add Request/Response Examples: At least one saved example response per request.
  • Write Tests (Assertions): Bruno test scripts asserting status codes.
  • CI/CD Integration (Optional): Bruno CLI (bru) in CI as automated test suite.
  • Static Site Generation: Generate static HTML API docs via Bruno CLI.

Source: docs/task/API_Documentation_Task_Plan.md

issues.display_title: Issue #371: [API Docs] Phase 1: Setup and Environment Configuration
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 29/06/26

✅ Implementation: Bruno API Documentation

This document provides a highly detailed, step-by-step implementation guide to execute the exhaustive tasks outlined in bruno-api-docs-tasks.md. It covers environment configuration, scripting, testing, and CI/CD integration.

This document provides a highly detailed, step-by-step implementation guide to execute the exhaustive tasks outlined in bruno-api-docs-tasks.md. It covers environment configuration, scripting, testing, and CI/CD integration using the Bruno OpenCollection YAML (.yml) format.

Phase 1: Setup and Environment Configuration

Before documenting individual endpoints, we must accurately map out the environments in Bruno.

1. Environment Setup

  1. Open Bruno and navigate to the Environments tab for the Panorra API Post collection.
  2. Ensure the following environments are properly configured:
    • Local - Dev (Variables: base_url = http://127.0.0.1:9999)
    • Dev (Variables: base_url = https://app2-dev.panorra.com)
    • Live (Variables: base_url = https://app2.panorra.com)
  3. Add a global variable token (leave the value blank initially) to store the dynamically extracted JWT/Bearer token.

2. Global Headers

  1. Open the Collection Settings in Bruno.
  2. Under the Headers tab, set default headers for all requests (unless overridden by specific requests):
    • Content-Type: application/json
    • Accept: application/json

3. Authentication Handling (Pre-request Script)

To avoid manual token entry across hundreds of endpoints, use Bruno's pre-request script feature at the root collection level.

  1. Open Collection Settings > Script > Pre Request.
  2. Add the following snippet:
const token = bru.getEnvVar("token");
if (token) {
  req.setHeader("Authorization", `Bearer ${token}`);
}

Bruno API documentation — based on docs/bruno-api-docs-tasks.txt

  • Initialize Bruno Collection: Verify and refine the opencollection.yml configuration.
  • Configure Environments: Ensure variables are mapped for the current environments (Local - Dev, Dev, and Live) inside the environments/ directory. Set up base URLs for each.
  • Global Setup: Configure global headers (e.g., Content-Type, Accept).
  • Authentication Handling: Set up pre-request scripts or auth configuration at the collection level to automatically inject Bearer tokens for protected routes.

issues.display_title: Issue #372: [API Docs] Phase 2: Core Identity and User Management
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 29/06/26

✅ Implementation: Bruno API Documentation

This document provides a highly detailed, step-by-step implementation guide to execute the exhaustive tasks outlined in bruno-api-docs-tasks.md. It covers environment configuration, scripting, testing, and CI/CD integration.

Phase 2: Core Identity and User Management

1. Auth Endpoints

The Auth module must be implemented first because other modules rely on the token generated here.

  • For endpoints like Login.yml, set the Post-response Script to extract the token: 
    const resData = res.getBody();
if (resData && resData.data && resData.data.token) {
  bru.setEnvVar("token", resData.data.token);
}

2. User Endpoints

  • Implement all endpoints under User/ including Account, Contact Us, Follow, Report Bug, etc.
  • File Uploads (e.g., Edit profile.yml): Set body type to Multipart Form, add a file key with the type set to File.

Bruno API documentation — based on docs/bruno-api-docs-tasks.txt

1. Auth Endpoints

  • Standard Login/Register: Login, Register User, Validate Register.
  • SSO / OAuth: Login Apple, Login Google, Register Apple, Register Google.
  • Other: Login Viewer, Token Search Address.

2. User Endpoints

  • Sub-modules: Account, Contact Us, Follow, Report Bug, Street View Account.
  • Profile & Status: Edit profile, Update password, Update Refresh Timeline, Welcome Back, Save Log.
  • Account Management: Deactive Account, Forgot Password, Reset Password.
  • OTP Management: Check OTP, OTP Delete, Resend OTP.
  • Moderation/Admin: Block User, Report User, Set All List User On Redis.
  • Integrations: Add Post Panorama GoThru, New Post Status.

issues.display_title: Issue #373: [API Docs] Phase 3: Main Features Documentation
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 29/06/26

✅ Implementation: Bruno API Documentation

This document provides a highly detailed, step-by-step implementation guide to execute the exhaustive tasks outlined in bruno-api-docs-tasks.md. It covers environment configuration, scripting, testing, and CI/CD integration.

Phase 3: Main Features Documentation

1. Post Endpoints

  • Implement all endpoints under Post/ including Draft, Folder Bookmark, Hashtag, Mention Post, Post Flow.
  • Path Variables: Ensure variables like {{post_id}} are used in URLs rather than hardcoded IDs.
  • Example Saving: After a successful test on Local - Dev, ensure you click Save Example in Bruno so the YAML file captures the JSON response payload.

2. Search Endpoints

  • Document the Search/History endpoints.
  • Ensure all query parameters (e.g. pagination) are documented natively in the Bruno Query tab.

3. Notification Endpoints

  • Document Notification/Broadcast and root notification endpoints.

Bruno API documentation — based on docs/bruno-api-docs-tasks.txt

1. Post Endpoints

  • Sub-modules: Best 360, Collection Post, Comment, Draft, Filter Custom, Folder Bookmark, Hashtag, Mention Post, Post Flow.
  • Root Post Endpoints: Block Post, Delete Post, Insert View Post, Like Post, Report Post, Update Post Video.

2. Search Endpoints

  • Sub-modules: History.

3. Notification Endpoints

  • Sub-modules: Broadcast.
  • Root Notification Endpoints: Push Notification, Read All Notification, Read Notification.

issues.display_title: Issue #375: [API Docs] Phase 5: Background Tasks and Support
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 29/06/26

✅ Implementation: Bruno API Documentation

This document provides a highly detailed, step-by-step implementation guide to execute the exhaustive tasks outlined in bruno-api-docs-tasks.md. It covers environment configuration, scripting, testing, and CI/CD integration.

Phase 5: Background Tasks and Support

1. Support Endpoints

  • Implement endpoints under Support/ (e.g., Ban, Category, Delete User).
  • Notes: If an endpoint requires an admin role or a specific server secret, document this in the endpoint's Notes tab.

2. Additional Endpoints

  • Implement Additional/Statistic, Additional/Version, and Device endpoints.

3. Cronjob Endpoints

  • Document all cleanup, messaging, and sync cronjobs.
  • If they bypass standard auth and use a specific header (e.g., X-Cron-Secret), add it to the request headers in Bruno.

Bruno API documentation — based on docs/bruno-api-docs-tasks.txt

1. Support Endpoints

  • Sub-modules: Ban, Category.
  • Root Support Endpoints: Add Affiliate Category, Add Affiliate Category Copy, Delete User, Login Support, Login as User.

2. Additional Endpoints

  • Sub-modules: Statistic, Version.
  • Root Additional Endpoints: Device.

3. Cronjob Endpoints

  • Data Cleanup: Clean File Attachments, Delete Draft Post, Delete Elastic, Delete User Containers, Delete User.
  • Messaging/Broadcast: Broadcast Like, Broadcast Post Subscribe, Message New Post.
  • Integrity Checks: Check Invalid Token Google, Check Verified Email.
  • Sync: Update Elastic.

issues.display_title: Issue #376: [API Docs] Phase 6: Enhancement and Automation
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 29/06/26

✅ Implementation: Bruno API Documentation

This document provides a highly detailed, step-by-step implementation guide to execute the exhaustive tasks outlined in bruno-api-docs-tasks.md. It covers environment configuration, scripting, testing, and CI/CD integration.

Phase 6: Enhancement and Automation

Once the documentation is robust and tests are written, integrate it into automated workflows.

1. Add Request/Response Examples

  • Verify that every .yml request in the OpenCollection has at least one saved example response to help frontend developers mock the API.

2. Write Tests (Assertions)

  • Add basic assertions in the Tests tab of Bruno to check standard success codes: 
    test("Status code is 200", function() {
  expect(res.getStatus()).to.equal(200);
});

3. CI/CD Integration

  • Install Bruno CLI: npm install -g @usebruno/cli
  • Run locally: bru run --env "Local - Dev"
  • Create a .github/workflows/bruno.yml for automated CI testing.

4. Static Site Generation

  • Generate static HTML API documentation using the CLI: 
    bru export --format html --output api-docs.html

Bruno API documentation — based on docs/bruno-api-docs-tasks.txt

  • Add Request/Response Examples: Ensure every request in Bruno has at least one saved example response to help frontend developers and testers.
  • Write Tests (Assertions): Write basic Bruno test scripts (test tab) to assert status codes (e.g., expect(res.getStatus()).to.equal(200)).
  • CI/CD Integration (Optional): Set up the Bruno CLI (bru) in the CI pipeline to run the collection as an automated test suite.
  • Static Site Generation: Generate static HTML API documentation using the Bruno CLI for public or internal sharing.

issues.display_title: Issue #377: Refactor Hashtag Module to Use GORM Models
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 29/06/26

✅ Implementation: Hashtag Module Refactoring

This document tracks the execution details for the Hashtag module refactoring.

Phase 1: HashtagPost.go

  • Target File: src/Action/V1/Hashtag/HashtagPost.go
  • Changes Applied:
    • Replaced manual post lookup query with Config.DB.Select("id, visibility").Where(...).First(&Models.Post{}).
    • Replaced raw DELETE FROM hashtag with Config.DB.Where(...).Delete(&Models.Hashtag{}).
    • Replaced raw INSERT INTO hashtag with bulk inserts via Config.DB.Create(&hashtagsToInsert).
    • Assigned unique UUID using uuid.New() natively.
  • Status: Completed & Compiled Successfully.

Phase 2: DeleteHashtagPost.go

  • Target File: src/Action/V1/Hashtag/DeleteHashtagPost.go
  • Changes Applied:
    • Structured lookups with Models.Post.
    • Removed raw SELECT id FROM hashtag checks used for verifying hashtag existence.
    • Eliminated raw DELETE strings for standard ORM Config.DB.Where(...).Delete(&Models.Hashtag{}).
  • Status: Completed & Compiled Successfully.

Refactor task — based on docs/refactor_hashtag.md

Background

The Hashtag module currently manages hashtag creation and deletion using raw SQL queries and generic map[string]interface{} bindings. Migrating this module to the new GORM models will provide compile-time safety, easier relational parsing, and proper zero-value protections.

Objective

Refactor the API endpoints within src/Action/V1/Hashtag/ to use structured GORM models (Models.Hashtag, Models.Post).

APIs to Refactor

  • HashtagPost: Refactor raw insertions and map lookups. Replace INSERT INTO and DELETE FROM with GORM's .Create() and .Delete().
  • DeleteHashtagPost: Refactor DELETE FROM to Config.DB.Delete(&Models.Hashtag{}).

Acceptance Criteria

  • No raw SQL INSERT or DELETE queries remain in the refactored endpoints.
  • Untyped map[string]interface{} logic used for reading DB results is replaced with structured GORM model types (Models.Post).
  • Explicitly generate and assign UUIDs using uuid.New() in Go before insertion to prevent zero-value constraint errors.
  • The code successfully compiles (go build) in the module directory.
